clean_story: Learning by doing.  Masters teach, novices earn. "Apprenticeship" comes from the Old French aprentis, meaning "to learn"—because knowledge sticks best when hands move and mistakes happen in real time. It’s like learning to ride a bike by wobbling behind someone who steadies you—except the bike might be a forge, a codebase, or a surgical tool. The model is ancient:Medieval guilds bound apprentices for 7 years to master a craft—blacksmithing, weaving—trading labor for skill. Today, Germany’s dual system splits time between classrooms and factories, producing mechanics and engineers with 90% employment rates. Key mechanism:The 4:1 ratio—one master guides four learners, ensuring mistakes are caught early. No hypotheticals. A chef’s apprentice doesn’t watch videos on julienning—they ruin 20 carrots while the chef corrects their grip. Modern twists:Tech startups use "apprenticeships" to train coders in 3 months via paid projects. Swiss watchmakers still require 4,000 hours of benchwork before touching a Patek Philippe movement. Three related subjects are skill acquisition, guild histories, and mentor ratios.
